2603.25524.title: CHIRP dataset: towards long-term, individual-level, behavioral monitoring of bird populations in the wild
2603.25524.paper_summary: Long-term behavioral monitoring of individual animals is crucial for studying behavioral changes that occur over different time scales, especially for conservation and evolutionary biology. Computer vision methods have proven to benefit biodiversity monitoring, but automated behavior monitoring in wild populations remains challenging. This stems from the lack of datasets that cover a range of computer vision tasks necessary to extract biologically meaningful measurements of individual animals. Here, we introduce such a dataset (CHIRP) with a new method (CORVID) for individual re-identification of wild birds. The CHIRP (Combining beHaviour, Individual Re-identification and Postures) dataset is curated from a long-term population of wild Siberian jays studied in Swedish Lapland, supporting re-identification (re-id), action recognition, 2D keypoint estimation, object detection, and instance segmentation. In addition to traditional task-specific benchmarking, we introduce application-specific benchmarking with biologically relevant metrics (feeding rates, co-occurrence rates) to evaluate the performance of models in real-world use cases. Finally, we present CORVID (COlouR-based Video re-ID), a novel pipeline for individual identification of birds based on the segmentation and classification of colored leg rings, a widespread approach for visual identification of individual birds. CORVID offers a probability-based id tracking method by matching the detected combination of color rings with a database. We use application-specific benchmarking to show that CORVID outperforms state-of-the-art re-id methods. We hope this work offers the community a blueprint for curating real-world datasets from ethically approved biological studies to bridge the gap between computer vision research and biological applications.
2603.25524.paper_summary_zh: 長期監測個體動物的行為對於研究在不同時間尺度上發生的行為變化至關重要，尤其是在保育和進化生物學領域。計算機視覺方法已被證明對生物多樣性監測有益，但在野生種群中自動行為監測仍然面臨挑戰。這源於缺乏涵蓋一系列計算機視覺任務的數據集，這些任務對於提取個體動物的生物學上有意義的測量是必要的。在此，我們介紹了一個這樣的數據集（CHIRP）及一種新方法（CORVID），用於野生鳥類的個體重新識別。CHIRP（結合行為、個體重新識別和姿勢）數據集是從在瑞典拉普蘭研究的長期野生西伯利亞松鴉種群中整理而來，支持重新識別（re-id）、行為識別、2D 關鍵點估計、物體檢測和實例分割。除了傳統的任務特定基準測試外，我們還引入了應用特定的基準測試，使用生物學相關的指標（進食率、共現率）來評估模型在現實世界用例中的表現。最後，我們介紹了CORVID（基於顏色的視頻重新識別），這是一種基於彩色腿環的分割和分類的鳥類個體識別新管道，這是一種廣泛使用的個體鳥類視覺識別方法。CORVID通過將檢測到的顏色環的組合與數據庫匹配，提供了一種基於概率的識別追蹤方法。我們使用應用特定的基準測試來顯示CORVID的表現超過了最先進的重新識別方法。我們希望這項工作能為社群提供一個藍圖，以從經過倫理批准的生物學研究中整理現實世界數據集，彌合計算機視覺研究與生物學應用之間的差距。
2603.25524.author: Alex Hoi Hang Chan et.al.
2603.25524.authors: Alex Hoi Hang Chan, Neha Singhal, Onur Kocahan, Andrea Meltzer, Saverio Lubrano, Miyako H. Warrington, Michel Griesser, Fumihiro Kano, Hemal Naik
